title: How a Forgotten Wire Turned a Cheap Chip Into a Brainlike Neuron
gist: Researchers at KAUST (Mario Lanza, Sebastian Pazos) accidentally discovered that an ordinary, decades-old 180nm CMOS transistor can behave as a spiking artificial neuron or an adjustable-conductance synapse when its normally-grounded fourth ('bulk') terminal is left floating or resistively tuned, exploiting a hidden parasitic bipolar-junction transistor inside every MOSFET. The resulting single-device 'NSRAM' neuron/synapse needs only 1-2 transistors versus dozens-to-hundreds in conventional neuromorphic circuits, is fully compatible with existing silicon fabs, achieved 100% yield across two different foundries, and survived over 10 million test cycles with zero failures.

insights.claim: Data-center GPUs used for AI inference and training consume as much as 1,000 watts each, roughly 1,000x a modern smartphone's power draw, and run continuously rather than intermittently like household appliances of comparable wattage.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Quantifies the power-per-chip baseline that neuromorphic and efficiency-focused compute startups are trying to undercut; useful denominator for evaluating claimed efficiency-gain multiples in AI hardware pitches.
insights.quote: each of those GPUs achieves that by consuming as much as 1,000 watts apiece
insights.locator: opening paragraph, 'Today, you probably asked a question'

insights.claim: The human brain is roughly one million times more energy-efficient than current AI systems at comparable cognitive tasks, framed by the authors as the benchmark neuromorphic engineering is chasing.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Sets the theoretical ceiling for how much upside exists in brain-inspired hardware efficiency plays, useful for sizing the addressable efficiency gap relative to GPU-based compute.
insights.quote: The brain is roughly one million times as energy efficient at many of the comparable tasks we set for AI.
insights.locator: 'The brain is roughly one million times'

insights.claim: GPU-based AI inefficiency stems fundamentally from software simulating neural networks on billions of general-purpose transistors, which forces energy-costly movement of massive data volumes rather than computing where data resides.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Explains why simply shrinking process nodes won't close the efficiency gap with the brain; the bottleneck is architectural (von Neumann data movement), not just transistor density, which favors in-memory/neuromorphic architectures over pure scaling.
insights.locator: 'Fundamentally, a lot of this inefficiency is because GPUs'

insights.claim: Existing state-of-the-art brain-inspired (neuromorphic) microchips already achieve up to 1,000x power reduction versus GPUs/CPUs on tasks like audio processing, computer vision, and health monitoring, despite being built from inefficient multi-transistor neuron/synapse circuits.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Shows neuromorphic efficiency gains are already proven at the chip level even before this discovery, meaning a device-level simplification (this MOSFET trick) compounds an already-validated efficiency thesis rather than being purely speculative.
insights.quote: state-of-the-art brain-inspired microchips have achieved power reductions up to a thousandfold compared with those of GPUs or CPUs on the same task
insights.locator: end of 'Biological and artificial neurons' section

insights.claim: In 2024 at Mario Lanza's lab, a student measuring a one-transistor-one-memristor memory circuit forgot to connect the MOSFET's bulk terminal, producing an unexpected sharp, nonlinear current spike with hysteresis that resembled neuron firing behavior.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: A textbook case of serendipitous discovery in hardware research; illustrates that basic overlooked terminals/wiring on 25-year-old fabrication tech can still yield novel device physics, a reminder not to dismiss mature process nodes (180nm) as innovation-exhausted.
insights.quote: One day the student forgot to connect the bulk terminal of the transistor.

insights.claim: The neuron-like behavior was traced to a hidden, unintentional bipolar-junction transistor that exists inside every standard MOSFET, activated via the normally-grounded fourth 'bulk' terminal.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for hardware investors/engineers: standard CMOS devices may harbor exploitable parasitic structures that unlock new functionality without new materials or process changes, a potentially generalizable source of low-cost innovation in mature fabs.
insights.quote: The unusual action the authors discovered is understandable if you consider that a MOSFET contains a hidden bipolar-junction transistor.

insights.claim: The devices were fabricated on a 180-nanometer commercial foundry node that was cutting-edge in the year 2000, meaning this neuromorphic behavior was found in what is now a legacy, low-cost, high-yield process rather than an advanced node.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Signals this technology could be manufactured cheaply at trailing-edge fabs with abundant global capacity, sidestepping the leading-edge (3nm/2nm) capacity bottlenecks and geopolitical chokepoints (TSMC, ASML EUV) that dominate AI chip supply chain risk.
insights.quote: The MOSFETs were created in a commercial foundry using fabrication technology called the 180-nanometer node, which was cutting-edge in the year 2000.

insights.claim: Lanza tasked postdoctoral fellow Sebastian Pazos with replicating the phenomenon in memristor-free chips using voltage pulses (mimicking neuron spikes) rather than the ramped voltage used in the original accidental observation, which clarified the underlying mechanism.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Shows the deliberate follow-up experimental design step that converted a lab accident into a controlled, reproducible phenomenon, the kind of methodological rigor that de-risks the discovery for patenting and publication.
insights.locator: 'After a fruitless week of trying to think of an explanation'

insights.claim: When the bulk terminal is floating, holes generated by impact ionization (electron-hole pairs from carriers colliding with silicon atoms) cannot drain to ground and instead accumulate, raising bulk voltage until it crosses a threshold (~0.7V, device-dependent) that activates the hidden bipolar transistor and triggers an abrupt current spike.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: This is the causal chain underlying the entire neuron-mimicking mechanism; understanding it is prerequisite for any competitor or licensee to replicate or design around the patent.
insights.quote: Once it reaches the threshold voltage, the device becomes sharply conductive, producing an abrupt increase of current.
insights.locator: 'To get current flowing through a bipolar transistor'

insights.claim: Adjusting the bulk terminal's resistance using a second MOSFET caused all tested transistors to fire at the same voltage threshold with hardly any variability, converting an inconsistent phenomenon into a controllable, uniform one.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Device-to-device variability is historically the reason experimental memristor/neuromorphic devices failed to scale into reliable manufacturable systems; solving it with a simple resistance-setting trick removes a major commercialization barrier.
insights.quote: Simply setting that resistance suddenly caused all the transistors to fire at the same voltage with hardly any variability.

insights.claim: The same physical mechanism (impact ionization creating charge that gets trapped in the gate dielectric) allows a MOSFET to also function as an artificial synapse: negative bulk-source voltage drives electrons into the dielectric to decrease conductance, positive voltage pushes holes in to increase it, giving a stable, electronically adjustable conductance state.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: A single transistor type now serves as both the 'neuron' and 'synapse' building blocks of a neural circuit, drastically simplifying the bill of materials for neuromorphic chip design.
insights.quote: A negative bulk-source voltage drives electrons into the dielectric, decreasing conductance. A positive one pushes holes in, increasing it.

insights.claim: Conventional neuromorphic implementations require interconnecting dozens to hundreds of CMOS transistors (plus bulky capacitors) to emulate a single neuron or synapse, which severely limits system scale; the new single/dual-transistor NSRAM approach collapses this component count by roughly two orders of magnitude.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Component-count reduction of this magnitude directly translates into die-area and cost reduction, a concrete lever for evaluating manufacturability claims versus other neuromorphic/memristor startups still requiring exotic materials.
insights.quote: each neuron and each synapse has been implemented by interconnecting dozens and sometimes even hundreds of MOSFETs, which is highly inefficient in terms of area, performance, and cost
insights.locator: 'The MOSFET Neuron' section, describing prior art

insights.claim: Every single device tested by the researchers operated successfully over 10 million cycles with zero failures, an unusually strong reliability result for an emerging device technology.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Reliability at this scale (10M cycles, 0 failures) is the kind of endurance data investors should look for before believing lab-stage neuromorphic claims can reach production; this figure is unusually strong versus typical early-stage memristor endurance numbers.
insights.quote: every single one of the devices we tested worked over 10 million cycles. Not even one of them failed during our tests.

insights.claim: The researchers deliberately re-tested the phenomenon on microchips from a different foundry to rule out a fab-specific artifact, and achieved the same behaviors with perfect yield again.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Cross-foundry reproducibility with 100% yield substantially de-risks the technology from a manufacturing-transfer standpoint, a key diligence question for any licensing or fab-partnership deal.
insights.quote: This result was so shocking that we decided to confirm it using microchips from a different foundry. It was successful: All the behaviors could be reproduced, and perfect yield was achieved once again.
insights.locator: 'The MOSFET Synapse' section, 'To be honest, we were amazed'

insights.claim: Dozens of research groups and companies have collectively spent many millions of dollars over the past 20 years trying to emulate neural behaviors using experimental memristor-like devices, largely without commercial success due to reliability and cost issues.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Positions the MOSFET-based discovery as a contrarian shortcut that bypasses two decades of costly, less-successful memristor R&D spend, an implicit argument that the industry has been over-investing in exotic materials when a standard transistor sufficed.
insights.quote: Dozens of research groups and companies all around the world have spent many millions of U.S. dollars over the past 20 years trying to emulate these neural behaviors using experimental memristor-like devices and other things, with limited success

insights.claim: The team began filing a patent and preparing a submission to the journal Nature for the neuron discovery before separately discovering the synapse behavior in the same device family.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: A pending patent filing signals intent to commercialize/license the IP; investors tracking neuromorphic-hardware IP landscapes should watch for the patent grant and any resulting licensing deals or spinouts tied to Lanza/Pazos.
insights.quote: we decided to confirm it using microchips from a different foundry... we had started the process of filing for a patent and writing up our findings for the journal Nature

insights.claim: The single-transistor neuron/synapse design (NSRAM) is fully compatible with existing silicon manufacturing lines, requiring no new materials or exotic fabrication steps, unlike memristor-based neuromorphic approaches.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Fab-compatibility is the single biggest determinant of time-to-market for semiconductor IP; this positions NSRAM technology for potentially fast adoption/licensing by existing chipmakers versus years-long qualification cycles for novel-material devices.
insights.quote: unlike other emerging technologies, our solution is fully compatible with today's silicon manufacturing lines and exhibits a yield of 100 percent in key figures of merit with near-zero variability
insights.locator: 'From neuromorphic device to circuit to system' section

insights.claim: The authors expect these brain-inspired chips to first find commercial fit in smaller-scale edge-AI applications, such as adding intelligence to battery-powered devices, before potentially scaling to compete with state-of-the-art GPUs.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: Sets realistic near-term commercial targeting (edge/IoT/wearables) rather than data-center GPU displacement, useful for investors calibrating time horizon and addressable market size before assuming this threatens Nvidia-scale compute demand.
insights.quote: These chips will first be a fit for smaller-scale, "edge-AI" tasks, such as bringing greater intelligence to battery-powered systems. But if we can scale up such chips, maybe in the long run they can compete with state-of-the-art GPUs.

insights.claim: Significant engineering work remains before production-ready circuits: improving computer models of the two devices' behavior, running circuit- and system-level simulations to validate architectures, designing peripheral driver/converter circuitry, and multiple fabrication rounds to optimize performance.
insights.speaker: Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos
insights.so_what: A concrete checklist of remaining technical milestones investors/partners can use to track maturation progress and estimate realistic time-to-commercial-product for this specific technology.
insights.quote: We must also perform accurate circuit- and system-level simulations to validate computing architectures, design peripheral circuitry to drive and convert signals, and undergo multiple fabrication rounds to optimize performance.
